narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges in key academic areas that the admissions model scrutinizes closely. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 sits below the typical 3.21 average, and your LSAT score of 26.50 is notably lower than the dataset average of 36.70, while your Year 1 school decile ranking of 6.00 is slightly above the average of 5.71. However, your Year 3 decile ranking dropped sharply to 1.00—far below the average of 5.53—which signaled a significant decline in performance. The model predicts a 56% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on a few standout factors from your profile, with your low Year 3 decile ranking emerging as the single most influential one, strongly pushing the model toward expecting bar failure due to that late-stage academic struggle. In contrast, your stronger Year 1 decile ranking worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success and showing early potential. Your LSAT score also played a notable role, nudging the outlook toward failure since it falls well short of what's typical for students who pass the bar. Compared to most applicants—who are overwhelmingly full-time students (92.7%)—your part-time status didn't sway things much here, but the model weighed your grades and test scores heaviest. Overall, your trajectory from a decent start to a weak finish in school, combined with that lower LSAT, set you apart from the stronger profiles that typically predict bar passage. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 36.70 average, and consider programs or tutoring to rebuild your academic momentum, perhaps documenting any extenuating circumstances from Year 3. Strengthening these areas could flip the model's prediction in your favor for future applications, giving you a clearer path to admission.
